candidate.test_code:
rows = [[1], [], [], [2]]
result = report(rows)["kept"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == [[1], [2]], "every blank row should be dropped"
candidate.context_note: Cleans a batch of parsed spreadsheet rows before they are rendered.

reason_options.text: The list is shortened while it is being walked, so the element that shifts into the vacated position is skipped
remove() deletes every matching element, not just the first one
enumerate() caches the length of the list before the loop starts
An empty list is truthy, so the guard inside the loop never runs

draft_explanation.summary: Deleting an element from a list while a for-loop is walking it shifts every later element down one position, but the loop's internal counter still advances. Whatever slides into the vacated index is never visited.
draft_explanation.principle: Never mutate a sequence while iterating over it. Iterate over a copy, or build a new list.

line_notes.note: This walks the same list that the body shortens.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: Two or more blank rows sit next to each other.
candidate.divergence_input: rows = [[1], [], [], [2]] passed to report(), reading the "kept" entry.
candidate.divergence_justification: With two adjacent blank rows, removing the first shifts the second down into the index the loop has already passed, so it survives. Walking a snapshot of the list visits both.

candidate.test_code:
result = resolve_all({"retries": 0, "verbose": False})
print(repr(result))
assert result == {"retries": 0, "timeout": 30, "verbose": False}, "an explicit zero should survive"
candidate.context_note: Merges a caller's overrides onto the service defaults.

reason_options.text: dict.get() raises KeyError when the key is absent, so the guard never runs
0 and False are falsy, so a value the caller supplied on purpose is discarded and replaced by the default
Iterating a dictionary yields its values, so resolve() receives the wrong argument
DEFAULT_SETTINGS is mutated by resolve(), so later lookups see stale entries

draft_explanation.summary: An override of 0 or False is a legitimate value, but both are falsy. Testing the value for truthiness cannot tell 'the caller said zero' apart from 'the caller said nothing', so a deliberate override is silently overwritten by the default.
draft_explanation.principle: To ask whether a value was supplied, compare against None. Truthiness answers a different question.

line_notes.note: This asks whether the value is truthy, not whether the caller supplied one.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: An override whose value is 0, False, or an empty string.
candidate.divergence_justification: The caller explicitly asked for zero retries and no verbosity. The truthiness test treats both as absent and substitutes the defaults; the None test keeps them.

candidate.test_code:
result = build_index(["  Alpha One  "])
print(repr(result))
assert result == {"alpha-one": 0}, "surrounding whitespace should be removed"
candidate.context_note: Turns human-typed category names into stable lookup keys.

reason_options.text: replace() only substitutes the first occurrence unless a count is given
lower() rewrites the string in place, so the following call sees the original text
enumerate() starts at 1, so every stored position is shifted by one
Strings are immutable, so strip() hands back a new string and the result of that call is thrown away

draft_explanation.summary: Every string method returns a new string and leaves the original untouched. Calling strip() as a bare statement computes a trimmed copy and immediately discards it, so the padding survives into the following two steps.
draft_explanation.principle: String methods are pure. Their return value is the only thing that changed.

line_notes.note: This computes a trimmed copy and throws it away.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A label that carries leading or trailing whitespace.
candidate.divergence_justification: The padding survives strip(), so the later replace() converts each surviving space into a dash and the key gains four dashes it should not have.

candidate.test_code:
events = [
    {"endpoint": "/alpha"},
    {"endpoint": "/alpha"},
    {"endpoint": "/alpha"},
    {"endpoint": "/zulu"},
]
result = summary(events)["busiest"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == "/alpha", "the endpoint with the most hits should win"
candidate.context_note: Picks the hottest endpoint from a window of access-log entries.

reason_options.text: reverse=True sorts ascending, so the smallest count ends up first
dict.items() yields keys only, so the lambda receives a string
The sort key reads the endpoint name instead of the hit count, so the ranking is alphabetical
counts.get(name, 0) resets the running count on every hit

draft_explanation.summary: items() yields (endpoint, count) pairs. Indexing the pair at 0 selects the endpoint, so the descending sort ranks the endpoints alphabetically and the winner is whichever name sorts last, not whichever endpoint was hit most.
draft_explanation.principle: A sort key must return the quantity you are ranking by, not merely something derived from the same record.

line_notes.note: Index 0 of the pair is the endpoint name; the count sits at index 1.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: The alphabetically last endpoint is not the most frequent one.
candidate.divergence_input: Four events: three hits on "/alpha" and one on "/zulu".
candidate.divergence_justification: Ranking by name in reverse puts '/zulu' first even though it was hit once. Ranking by count puts '/alpha' first with three hits.

candidate.test_code:
result = audit(LINE_ITEMS, 0.3)["reconciles"]
print(repr(result))
assert result is True, "the line items should reconcile against the quoted total"
candidate.context_note: Checks a generated invoice against the total the customer was quoted.

reason_options.text: A tuple cannot be unpacked in a for-loop, so amount is bound to the whole pair
Binary floating point cannot hold these decimals exactly, so the accumulated sum is not bit-identical to the expected value
total is initialised to 0.0 rather than 0, which forces integer truncation
line_total() is called twice, so the amount is doubled before the comparison

draft_explanation.summary: 0.1 and 0.2 have no exact binary representation, so adding them yields 0.30000000000000004 rather than 0.3. Exact equality between two floats that were arrived at by different arithmetic almost never holds, even when the decimal maths agrees.
draft_explanation.principle: Compare floats within a tolerance chosen from the domain, never with equality.

line_notes.note: Two floats that agree in decimal can still differ in the last bit.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: Any total assembled from decimals that binary floating point cannot represent exactly.
candidate.divergence_input: LINE_ITEMS of 0.1 and 0.2 checked against an expected total of 0.3.
candidate.divergence_justification: The accumulated sum is 0.30000000000000004, which is not equal to 0.3, so the exact comparison reports a mismatch. A tolerance of half a cent accepts it.

candidate.test_code:
result = locate("1,2;3,4;5,6", [3, 4])["position"]
print(repr(result))
assert result == 1, "the matching row should be found by value"
candidate.context_note: Finds where a given row sits inside a freshly parsed table.

reason_options.text: The identity operator asks whether two names point at the same object, and the parsed row is a different object from the target even though their contents match
enumerate() yields the value first and the index second, so position holds a list
split(";") discards the final chunk, so the target row is never inspected
Two lists cannot be compared with the equality operator, which would raise TypeError

draft_explanation.summary: parse_rows() builds brand-new list objects. The caller's target is a separate object that merely holds the same numbers, so an identity test is False for every row and the search reports failure.
draft_explanation.principle: Identity asks 'the same object?'. Equality asks 'the same value?'. Data comparison almost always wants the second.

line_notes.note: This asks whether the two names refer to one object, not whether they hold the same values.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: The target holds the same values as a parsed row but is a distinct object.
candidate.divergence_justification: The literal [3, 4] handed in by the caller is not the same object as the [3, 4] that parse_rows() constructed, so the identity test never succeeds and the search falls through to its not-found result.

candidate.test_code:

print(repr(result))
assert result == "retries=3 timeout=30", "a later call should not inherit an earlier override"
candidate.context_note: Layers a caller's settings on top of the service defaults before a run.

reason_options.text: dict.update() returns a new dictionary, so the return value is discarded
sorted() on a dictionary yields its values, so describe() reads the wrong side of each pair
The second call passes an empty dictionary, so with_overrides() returns None
The assignment binds a second name to the module-level dictionary rather than copying it, so every call permanently rewrites the shared defaults

draft_explanation.summary: Assignment in Python binds a name; it does not copy. config and BASE_CONFIG therefore refer to one dictionary, and update() rewrites it in place. The first caller's override becomes the new default for everyone who follows.
draft_explanation.principle: Assignment binds, it does not copy. To leave a shared structure intact, copy it before mutating.

line_notes.note: This makes config a second name for the module-level dictionary.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A second call that relies on a default the first call overrode.
candidate.divergence_input: run_twice({"retries": 9}, {}) - an override of 9 retries followed by a call that overrides nothing.
candidate.divergence_justification: The first call writes 9 into the shared defaults, so the second call, which overrides nothing, still reports 9. Copying before the update leaves the module-level dictionary untouched.

candidate.test_code:
result = pack([{"label": "caf\u00e9-latte"}])[0]["fits"]
print(repr(result))
assert result is False, "the label overruns the field once it is encoded"
candidate.context_note: Prepares labels for a binary record format with a byte budget per field.

reason_options.text: encode() rewrites the string in place, so len() already sees the encoded form
len() on a string counts characters, but the field budget is measured in bytes, and a non-ASCII character costs more than one byte
UTF-8 spends exactly two bytes on every character, so the count is always double what len() reports
The record dictionary is rebuilt on each pass, so only the last label is ever packed

draft_explanation.summary: A str is a sequence of characters; the record format budgets bytes. UTF-8 spends one byte on ASCII but two or more on anything else, so a label can sit inside the character budget and still overrun the field once encoded.
draft_explanation.principle: Characters and bytes are different units. Measure in whichever unit the destination is counting.

line_notes.note: This counts characters, but the destination counts bytes.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A label containing a non-ASCII character whose character count sits at the limit.
candidate.divergence_input: A single record whose label is the ten-character string cafe-latte with an accented e, which is eleven bytes in UTF-8.
candidate.divergence_justification: The label is ten characters, so the character count clears a ten-unit budget. Encoded it is eleven bytes, which does not, and the record would be silently truncated downstream.

candidate.test_code:
accounts = open_accounts(["ada", "linus"])
accounts[0].ledger.add(50)
result = accounts[1].ledger.balance()
print(repr(result))
assert result == 0, "a freshly opened account should start empty"
candidate.context_note: Opens ledger-backed accounts for a batch of new customers.

reason_options.text: sum() on an empty list raises TypeError, so balance() never returns a number
A dataclass copies each default value for every instance, so the two ledgers are already independent
The default is a single Ledger object built once when the class body is executed, so every account shares it
open_accounts() appends the same Account object twice because the loop variable is rebound

draft_explanation.summary: A dataclass field default is an ordinary expression, evaluated once while the class body runs. One Ledger is built at import time and handed to every Account that does not supply its own, so all accounts share a single balance.
draft_explanation.principle: A default value is created once, at definition time. For anything mutable, use default_factory so each instance gets its own.

line_notes.note: This Ledger is constructed once, when the class is defined, and reused by every instance.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: A second account is opened without an explicit ledger and the first one has already been posted to.
candidate.divergence_input: open_accounts(["ada", "linus"]), posting 50 to the first account and reading the second account balance.
candidate.divergence_justification: Both accounts hold a reference to the same Ledger built at class-definition time, so the posting to the first is visible in the second. default_factory constructs a fresh Ledger per instance.

candidate.test_code:
result = paginate(["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"])
print(repr(result))
assert result == [["a", "b", "c"], ["d", "e", "f"]], "every item should appear on exactly one page"
candidate.context_note: Splits a result set into equal-size pages for the API response.

reason_options.text: A slice already excludes its end index, so subtracting one more drops the last item of every page
divmod() hands back the remainder first, so page_count() is computed from the wrong half of the pair
range() includes its upper bound, so one page too many is produced
Slicing a list with a start index beyond its length raises IndexError

draft_explanation.summary: A slice is already half-open: items[0:3] yields three items and stops before index 3. Subtracting one from the end index therefore takes PAGE_SIZE minus one items per page, and one record per page never reaches the caller.
draft_explanation.principle: Python slices exclude their upper bound. The end index is a stop, not a last index.

line_notes.note: The slice already stops before this index, so the subtraction removes a second item.

candidate.bug_trigger_condition: Any page that is full, which is every page except possibly the last.
candidate.divergence_input: paginate(["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"]) with a page size of three.
candidate.divergence_justification: Each page takes items[start:start+2] instead of items[start:start+3], so 'c' and 'f' are silently lost while page_count still reports two pages.
